title: Text Is Not All You Need: Multimodal Prompting Helps LLMs Understand Humor
research_questions: Does providing an LLM with both the text of a joke and a TTS-generated spoken version improve humor understanding and explanation compared with text-only prompting?
Does audio input help preserve phonetic ambiguity that is important for understanding puns?
How do multimodal prompt configurations and TTS voice choices affect humor explanation quality?

methods_proposed.name: TTS-based multimodal prompting
methods_proposed.summary: The method converts each text joke into audio using OpenAI’s tts-1-hd and prompts an audio-capable LLM with both the original text and the spoken form. The prompt includes task definitions, six few-shot pun explanation examples with chain-of-thought reasoning, and instructions not to mention the input modality.

methods_proposed.name: Multimodal aggregation prompting
methods_proposed.summary: The paper tests a setup where text-only and audio-only explanations are generated separately and then combined through an aggregation prompt. The paper reports that a single combined audio-text prompt outperformed this separate strategy and was used going forward.

evaluation_methods.detail: GPT-4o judged pairwise explanation quality. For SemEval, Gemini-1.5-Flash explanations were compared against human explanations; for Context-Situated Puns and ExplainTheJoke, baseline text-only and multimodal outputs were compared directly. Pairwise comparisons were run twice with swapped order to mitigate positional bias, and results were reported as win and tie rates.
The appendix analyzes transcription logits from Gazelle v0.2 to test whether audio preserves phonetic ambiguity, including the word “Where” and the pun “Patience is a heavy weight.”

headline_results.finding: On SemEval heterographic puns, multimodal prompting outperformed the text-only baseline when judged against human explanations.
headline_results.value: with audio: 51.74 Win %, 4.56 Tie %; Baseline: 47.76 Win %, 5.64 Tie %

headline_results.finding: On SemEval homographic puns, multimodal prompting outperformed the text-only baseline when judged against human explanations.
headline_results.value: with audio: 72.59 Win %, 6.36 Tie %; Baseline: 68.89 Win %, 8.40 Tie %

headline_results.finding: On Context-Situated Puns heterographs, multimodal prompting was preferred more often than the text-only baseline in direct pairwise comparison.
headline_results.value: with audio: 36.49 Win %; Baseline: 33.87 Win %; Tie %: 29.65

headline_results.finding: On Context-Situated Puns homographs, multimodal prompting was preferred more often than the text-only baseline in direct pairwise comparison.
headline_results.value: with audio: 36.85 Win %; Baseline: 35.08 Win %; Tie %: 28.08

headline_results.finding: On ExplainTheJoke, multimodal prompting outperformed the text-only baseline despite many ties.
headline_results.value: with audio: 15.44 Win %; Baseline: 12.81 Win %; Tie %: 71.75

headline_results.finding: In the SemEval ablation, audio-only prompting performed far worse than the full system.
headline_results.value: No text, Audio Only: 25.50 heterograph, 55.86 homograph; Our System: 51.74 heterograph, 72.59 homograph

key_findings: Providing both text and TTS-generated audio improved explanation quality across all three evaluated datasets.
On SemEval, multimodal prompting increased win rates over the text-only baseline for both heterographic puns and homographic puns when judged against human explanations.
On Context-Situated Puns, where no human explanations were available, GPT-4o preferred multimodal outputs over text-only outputs more often for both heterographs and homographs.
On ExplainTheJoke, multimodal prompting still improved direct pairwise win rate, suggesting benefits beyond phonetic ambiguity in puns.
Audio-only prompting was substantially worse than the combined text-audio system, indicating that audio should supplement rather than replace text.

The logit analysis showed that audio transcription assigned high probability to homophones such as “wear” and “ware” for “Where,” and made “weight” and “wait” the top two tokens in a realistic pun scenario.
limitations: The approach is highly prompt-sensitive, and small variations in phrasing or structure can produce significant differences in output quality.
The TTS-based approach does not fully capture timing, cadence, rhythm, and other subtleties of real human speech.
The evaluation relies on automated LLM-based judging, which may not fully capture the nuanced quality of humor explanations.
The paper notes that future work should include more robust evaluation strategies such as human assessments or stronger judge models as they are released.
The study leaves open deeper investigation of voice characteristics and additional modalities such as video.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper states that large language models can produce offensive and incorrect statements, and that explaining comedy may involve harmful stereotypes and offensive content; users should exercise caution and avoid prompting the LLM with potentially offensive jokes to avoid perpetuating incorrect stereotypes. The work is released for research purposes only.

artifacts.other: Prompts are included in Appendix A.

human_samples.observations: SemEval 2017 Task 7 contains 810 homographic puns, 647 heterographic puns, and 1077 non-puns; each pun has human annotations of the pun-word and the spelling or definition in both interpretations, plus a human-provided sentence-form explanation.
human_samples.detail: The paper uses pre-existing SemEval human annotations and explanations; it does not report annotator identities, counts, demographics, recruitment, or annotation protocol details beyond the dataset description.

human_samples.observations: Context-Situated Puns contains 821 homographic puns and 1739 heterographic puns with human annotations of the pun-word and spelling/definition information; it does not contain human explanations.
human_samples.detail: The paper uses pre-existing Context-Situated Puns annotations as ground-truth context for judging. It does not report annotator counts, demographics, recruitment, or geography.

human_samples.population: ExplainTheJoke.com contributors or authors, not otherwise described
human_samples.recruitment: Dataset was scraped from the ExplainTheJoke.com website.
human_samples.observations: ExplainTheJoke contains 350 jokes; each entry has paragraph-form human explanations with high variability in style, length, quality, and accuracy.
human_samples.detail: The paper uses pre-existing website explanations; it does not report contributor counts, demographics, recruitment beyond website scraping, or quality-control procedures.

judges.name: GPT-4o
judges.detail: Used as the pairwise judge for explanation quality. For SemEval, it compared each Gemini-1.5-Flash generated explanation against the dataset human explanation, with dataset annotations provided. For Context-Situated Puns and ExplainTheJoke, it directly compared baseline text-only and multimodal outputs. Pairwise comparisons were run twice with the explanation order swapped, and win rates were averaged to mitigate positional bias.

judges.name: GPT, version not specified
judges.detail: For ExplainTheJoke, the paper says it first asked GPT to summarize the provided human explanations; that summary was then used as relevant context for the judge-LLM. The exact GPT model/version and prompt are not reported.

judges.name: Gazelle v0.2
judges.detail: Used in Appendix A.2 for a diagnostic transcription-logit analysis of phonetic ambiguity because public LLM APIs did not provide logits. The paper inspected logits for the audio word “Where” and for the pun “Patience is a heavy weight.”

judges.name: SemEval 2017 Task 7 human annotators/explanation writers
judges.detail: Provided the pre-existing pun-word/spelling-or-definition annotations and sentence-form explanations used as ground-truth context and as the human comparison explanations in SemEval.

judges.name: Context-Situated Puns human annotators
judges.detail: Provided pre-existing pun-word and spelling/definition annotations used as ground-truth context for GPT-4o judging; no human explanations were available.

judges.name: ExplainTheJoke.com explanation authors
judges.detail: Provided pre-existing paragraph-form explanations that were summarized by an unspecified GPT model and used as judge context; they were not directly compared as a human baseline in the reported pairwise results.

generation_selection.scope: Main humor-explanation generation with Gemini-1.5-Flash on SemEval, Context-Situated Puns, and ExplainTheJoke under text-only baseline and combined text-plus-audio prompting
generation_selection.n: One reported generated explanation per item per prompting condition
generation_selection.detail: The paper reports fixed generated explanations being judged; it does not report generating multiple candidate explanations or selecting best-of-N outputs. The order-swapped GPT-4o judging is repeated evaluation of fixed outputs, not generation selection.

generation_selection.scope: Separate multimodal aggregation strategy shown in Figure 1-Left
generation_selection.n: One text-only explanation, one audio-only explanation, then one aggregation output per item in the described strategy
generation_selection.detail: This strategy was tested to combine separate modality-specific explanations into one final output, but the paper states it performed worse than the combined audio-text prompt and does not report detailed metrics or selection among multiple candidates.

generation_selection.scope: Ablation and voice-type experiments on SemEval
generation_selection.n: One reported output per item per ablation or TTS voice condition; the Onyx + Alloy condition passed two audio files along with text in one prompt
generation_selection.detail: The ablations compare audio-only prompting, removing aggregation wording, the main system, and TTS voices. No best-of-N generation or candidate-selection procedure is reported.

model_provenance.name: Gemini-1.5-Flash
model_provenance.detail: Used to generate humor/pun explanations. The paper states Gemini-1.5-Flash was chosen because, at the time, only the Gemini family offered API-based audio input and Flash balanced performance and affordability. No API snapshot, release variant, inference date, or decoding settings are reported.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4o
model_provenance.detail: Used as the LLM judge. The paper cites OpenAI 2024 and says GPT-4o was chosen as the strongest available model at the time and to avoid using the same model family for generation and evaluation. No snapshot or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: OpenAI tts-1-hd
model_provenance.detail: Used to convert text jokes into audio. Alloy was used throughout the main experiments; Nova, Onyx, Alloy, and Onyx + Alloy are compared in the voice analysis. No model snapshot, inference date, or TTS parameter settings beyond voice choice are reported.

model_provenance.name: Gazelle v0.2
model_provenance.detail: Open-source model available on Hugging Face, used for transcription-logit analysis in Appendix A.2. The paper does not report the Hugging Face revision, runtime environment, or inference date.

model_provenance.name: GPT, version not specified
model_provenance.detail: Used to summarize ExplainTheJoke human explanations before GPT-4o judging. The exact GPT model, provider interface, snapshot, prompt, and inference date are not reported.

inference_budgets.scope: TTS audio generation for multimodal prompts
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: One text-to-speech conversion per joke in the main Alloy condition; the Onyx + Alloy voice analysis used two audio files in the prompt
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: One audio file per joke in the main condition
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Voice choice is reported: Alloy for main experiments; Nova, Onyx, Alloy, and Onyx + Alloy in the voice analysis. No other TTS parameters are reported.
inference_budgets.detail: The paper states that no additional ground-truth emphasis or timing information was provided to the LLM.

inference_budgets.scope: Gemini-1.5-Flash explanation generation, main baseline and with-audio conditions
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: One generated explanation per item per reported prompting condition
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: One reported explanation per item per condition; no multiple candidates are reported
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: The paper says Gemini-1.5-Flash offered the best balance between performance and affordability; no actual cost is reported.
inference_budgets.detail: Prompt components included task definitions, instructions, chain-of-thought-style few-shot examples, and either text alone or text plus TTS audio. Exact max tokens, temperature, top-p, seed, retries, and safety settings are not reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Three LLM calls per item in the described setup: text-only explanation, audio-only explanation, and aggregation prompt
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: One final aggregated output; no best-of or selection among candidates is reported
inference_budgets.detail: The paper says this strategy was extensively tested and was worse than the combined audio-text strategy, but does not report the number of tests, datasets/items used, or detailed scores.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Two judge calls per pairwise comparison, with explanation order swapped
inference_budgets.detail: For SemEval, each model explanation was paired with the human explanation; for Context-Situated Puns and ExplainTheJoke, baseline and with-audio model outputs were compared directly. Dataset annotations or summarized human-explanation context were provided where applicable.
The paper reports that GPT summarized the provided human explanations before judging, but does not identify the model, prompt, number of calls, or decoding settings.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Two reported transcription-logit probes: the word “Where” and the pun “Patience is a heavy weight”
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: Top-token logits/probabilities inspected; no sampled candidate set is described
inference_budgets.detail: Used only for diagnostic analysis of whether audio preserved homophone alternatives such as where/wear/ware and weight/wait.

human_baselines.description: Pre-existing human-provided sentence-form explanations were paired with Gemini-1.5-Flash generated explanations and judged by GPT-4o; dataset annotations were provided to the judge.
human_baselines.detail: The same pun items were compared under the same GPT-4o pairwise judging procedure, but the human explanations were pre-existing dataset artifacts and the paper does not report that humans produced them under the same prompt, format constraints, or conditions as the model.

human_baselines.description: The dataset has human annotations but no human explanations; evaluation compares text-only and multimodal Gemini outputs directly.
human_baselines.detail: Human annotations supply ground-truth context for the judge but do not constitute a human explanation baseline.

human_baselines.description: The dataset includes paragraph-form human explanations, but the reported evaluation compares text-only and multimodal Gemini outputs directly after using a GPT-generated summary of the human explanations as judge context.
human_baselines.detail: The human explanations are not evaluated as a competing baseline and are described as variable in style, length, quality, and accuracy.

contamination_risk.basis: SemEval 2017 and Context-Situated Puns 2022 are public benchmarks predating Gemini-1.5-Flash and GPT-4o, and ExplainTheJoke is a scraped public web dataset. The paper reports no decontamination test, no held-out/private item construction, and no model-training-data audit. It also says the few-shot examples were selected from each dataset tested, but does not report whether these examples were excluded from evaluated items or whether a strict split was used. The generated audio is new TTS output, but it is derived from public source text and is not a convincing contamination control.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: The headline claims that multimodal prompting improves explanation quality across SemEval, Context-Situated Puns, ExplainTheJoke, ablations, and TTS voice comparisons rest on GPT-4o pairwise judgments. The phonetic-ambiguity mechanism claim is supported by Gazelle v0.2 logit analysis.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: No new human evaluation is reported. The study uses pre-existing human annotations/explanations as references or judge context for some datasets, but not human raters validating the reported model comparisons.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: All reported win/tie metrics are produced by LLM judging, with order swapping to mitigate positional bias. The paper itself lists automated LLM-based judging as an evaluation limitation and suggests future human assessment.

reporting_gaps: No participant or annotator counts, demographics, recruitment details, or geography are reported for the human annotations/explanations in the reused datasets.
No API snapshot/version, inference date, or exact provider configuration is reported for Gemini-1.5-Flash, GPT-4o, or OpenAI tts-1-hd.
The GPT model used to summarize ExplainTheJoke human explanations is not identified, and its summarization prompt and decoding settings are not reported.
No temperature, top-p, max-token limit, seed, retry policy, parsing-failure handling, or safety/filter settings are reported for generation or judging.
No exact API call totals, runtime, hardware, or monetary cost are reported, despite affordability motivating the Gemini-1.5-Flash choice.
The paper does not report a train/dev/test split, exact evaluated item IDs, or whether few-shot examples selected from each dataset were excluded from evaluation items.
No contamination or memorization analysis is reported for public benchmark/web data evaluated with later proprietary models.
No human validation, inter-rater comparison, or calibration study is reported for GPT-4o judgments on this task.
No confidence intervals, statistical significance tests, or effect-size uncertainty estimates are reported for the win-rate differences, even though the prose describes improvements as significant.
The scope and quantitative results of the discarded separate aggregation strategy are not reported beyond the statement that it was worse in every test performed.
TTS prosody, speed, cadence, volume, and other speech-generation parameters are not reported; only voice choice is described.
The Hugging Face revision, runtime environment, and full inference setup for Gazelle v0.2 are not reported.
